next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

If Nate looked at the great room from the right angle, Monday almost passed for any other draft weekend cleanup. The football was on mute. Empty bottles crowded the coffee table. Six men argued about rules Martin used to pretend he hated settling.

Joel ended that in under a minute.

"So are we dealing with the roster or what?"

Nobody answered.

"Martin's roster," Joel said. "Season starts Thursday. His team's sitting there. Somebody has to decide what happens."

Tommy was stretched out on one end of the couch, one ankle on his knee, beer balanced against his stomach.

"His team plays."

"That doesn't answer the question."

"It answers it fine."

Paul said, "I think Joel means bye weeks, injuries, waiver claims. The team still needs a manager."

Cliff lifted his beer from the armchair by the window.

"Let it auto-manage. Let the asset keep running. No need to get cute."

"Auto-manage is dogshit," Nate said. "Auto-manage started Dalton over Herbert last year. I did twelve minutes on it because I was furious and because no one in my real life respects boundaries."

Tommy pointed the bottle at him.
open_hooks_to_carry: Cliff approaches Tommy on the deck with two beers and a proposition
Cliff names the fire, names Ray Toomey, references the document
Cliff's ask: not cash, but Tommy's influence to recover the forty thousand Martin owed
Tommy's flashback to the night of the fire — the truck, the smoke, twenty-two years of managed guilt
Tommy agrees to Cliff's terms but something physical shifts — the jock bluster the reader has been trained to read as comedy is now legible as genuine danger
The comedy in this chapter should have a visible edge that the reader can feel but might not identify as lethal

payoffs_to_land.description: Cliff uses the document he discovered in Ch 8 to leverage Tommy. The financial motive that led Cliff to the safe has been replaced by a leverage play worth far more than forty thousand dollars.

prev_tail_excerpt:
...now what those terms were. He didn't know if the answer was guilt, debt, pity, or something messier than any of those. He only knew the same bend in Martin, the same extra care where Tommy was concerned, had survived long enough to reach the will.

He thought about the envelope again. RT. If the initials had anything to do with Tommy, the answer might be sitting twenty feet behind him in the study.

But not alone. Not with the safe already disturbed and the will still hot in him. If he opened it now, whatever he found would arrive already bent by jealousy, and if the envelope mattered, he would want another set of eyes on it from the first second.

So he sat. He watched the pontoon boat complete its slow circuit of the far shore. He listened to the refrigerator cycle on inside the house and the distant murmur of Nate's voice from around the corner, narrating something to no one. He thought about Martin's gap-toothed smile when he'd shown up with that chainsaw, proud and incompetent and absolutely certain he was helping.

Martin had been his best friend. Paul did not doubt this. Martin's friendship with Paul had been real, the conversations, the Christmases, the chainsaw, the potato salad compliments, the twenty-five years of showing up. All real. All genuine.

It just hadn't been the first loyalty in Martin's life. That belonged, somehow, to Tommy, and the will made it impossible to call that casual.

Paul sat in the dead man's chair and felt the specific, humiliating comedy of a man who had spent twenty-five years as the best friend and had only just learned he was second.
secondary_character_beats: Cliff's leverage play should feel like a real estate negotiation, not a villain's demand — he genuinely thinks he's being reasonable
state_deltas_required: Cliff leveraging the fire document against Tommy
Tommy is under direct external threat — his guilt about the Viagra was internal and manageable; Cliff's leverage is external, ongoing, and escalating. The murder motive is fully formed.
